description: Centralizing order numbering — review a migration that replaces a per-region SEQUENCE with a single shared counter row across a 500 orders/sec workload
review_checklist: Identify single-row contention bottlenecks under high write throughput
Verify lock semantics of UPDATE...RETURNING in PL/pgSQL functions
Check whether sequence drop has cascading effects on existing keys
Evaluate gap-tolerance and idempotency of the new ID strategy
Compare to alternatives: per-shard sequences, ULID, Snowflake IDs

ground_truth.description: Single-row UPDATE on global_counters serializes ALL inserts at 500 orders/sec — every transaction acquires a row-level lock on the same tuple, creating an O(n) queue. This is the textbook 'hot row' anti-pattern. Throughput will collapse from thousands/sec to ~100/sec under realistic concurrency.

UPDATE...RETURNING inside a PL/pgSQL function holds the row lock until commit, not until the function returns. Combined with any downstream work in the calling transaction, lock duration grows beyond a single statement.

DROP SEQUENCE orders_id_seq CASCADE detaches the default for orders.id, breaking all subsequent INSERTs that don't explicitly provide an id. The CASCADE silently drops dependent objects without enumeration.
DEFAULT expression calling next_order_number() executes per-row during INSERT — fine for normal traffic, but during bulk imports it serializes the entire batch on the global_counters row.

Architectural concern: shared global counter is the wrong primitive for cross-region uniqueness. Use Snowflake-style IDs (timestamp + shard_id + sequence) or ULID — both are k-sortable and avoid coordination entirely.
